Base-$b$ ramp digits with order-3 cycle test and exact $p$-adic/$q$-adic divisibility

Let $b=21297$, $p=11$, $q=13$, $k=3$, $L=77$, and $n=p^kq=11^3\cdot 13=17303$.
For each integer $t$ with $0\le t\le n-1$, define digits $d_i(t)=t+i$ for $i=0,1,\dots,L-1$ and define the base-$b$ integer
$$
N_t=\sum_{i=0}^{L-1} d_i(t)\,b^i=\sum_{i=0}^{76} (t+i)b^i.
$$
(Here $b\ge n+L$, so $0\le t+i\le n+L-2<b$ and all $d_i(t)$ are valid base-$b$ digits.)

For a prime $r$ and nonzero integer $X$, let $v_r(X)$ be the largest integer $e\ge 0$ such that $r^e\mid X$.

Let
$$
S=\{t\in\{0,1,\dots,n-1\}:\ v_{11}(N_t)=3\ \text{and}\ v_{13}(N_t)=1\}.
$$
Equivalently, $t\in S$ iff $n\mid N_t$ but $11^4\nmid N_t$ and $13^2\nmid N_t$.

Compute $|S|$ and output the unique integer $N\in\{0,1,\dots,999\}$ such that $N\equiv |S|\pmod{1000}$.
(Here the natural output is the integer $|S|$, so the normalization rule is $N\equiv |S|\ (\mathrm{mod}\ 1000)$.)

Solution.
Step 1 (positional_value_expansion): Define
$$
A=\sum_{i=0}^{76} b^i,\qquad B=\sum_{i=0}^{76} i\,b^i.
$$
Then
$$N_t=\sum_{i=0}^{76}(t+i)b^i=t\sum_{i=0}^{76}b^i+\sum_{i=0}^{76} i b^i=tA+B.$
This converts all congruence conditions on $N_t$ into linear congruences in $t$.

Step 2 (divisibility_via_digits): Since $11^3\mid (b-1)$, we have $b\equiv 1\pmod{11^3}$, hence $b^i\equiv 1\pmod{11^3}$ for all $i$.
So, using from Step 1,
$$
N_t\equiv \sum_{i=0}^{76}(t+i)=77t+\frac{76\cdot 77}{2}=77(t+38)\pmod{11^3}.
$$
(This is the same digit-sum mechanism as the classical casting-out-nines theorem mod_3_mod_9_digits.)

Step 3 (linear_congruence_solving): Because $77=11\cdot 7$ has $v_{11}(77)=1$, from Step 2 we get
$$
11^3\mid N_t\ \Longleftrightarrow\ 11^3\mid 77(t+38)\ \Longleftrightarrow\ 11^2\mid (t+38).
$$
Thus
$$
11^3\mid N_t\ \Longleftrightarrow\ t\equiv -38\pmod{121}.
$$
Equivalently modulo $11^3=1331$, the solutions are $t\equiv -38+121s\pmod{1331}$ for $s=0,1,\dots,10$.

Step 4 (cycle_decomposition_mod): Since $13\mid (b^2+b+1)$, we have $b^3-1=(b-1)(b^2+b+1)\equiv 0\pmod{13}$.
Also $b\equiv 21297\equiv 3\not\equiv 1\pmod{13}$, so $b\not\equiv 1\pmod{13}$ and hence $13\nmid (b-1)$.
Therefore, by the cyclotomic identity $x^3-1=(x-1)(x^2+x+1)$ (theorem cyclotomic_order_three_digits_test),
$$
b^3\equiv 1\pmod{13}\quad\text{and}\quad 1+b+b^2\equiv 0\pmod{13}.
$$
With $L=77=3\cdot 25+2$, a cycle-split by exponents $i\bmod 3$ gives
$$
A=\sum_{i=0}^{76} b^i\equiv 25(1+b+b^2)+(1+b)\equiv 1+b\pmod{13}.
$$
Similarly,
$$
B=\sum_{i=0}^{76} i b^i \equiv
\sum_{j=0}^{25}(3j)\cdot 1+\sum_{j=0}^{25}(3j+1)\cdot b+\sum_{j=0}^{24}(3j+2)\cdot b^2\pmod{13}.
$$
The first coefficient is $3\sum_{j=0}^{25}j=975\equiv 0\pmod{13}$ and the second is $\sum_{j=0}^{25}(3j+1)=1001\equiv 0\pmod{13}$, while the third is $\sum_{j=0}^{24}(3j+2)=950\equiv 1\pmod{13}$.
So $B\equiv b^2\pmod{13}$.
Using $b\equiv 3\pmod{13}$ gives $A\equiv 1+3=4$ and $B\equiv 3^2=9$, so from Step 1,
$$
N_t\equiv 4t+9\pmod{13}.
$$

Step 5 (linear_congruence_solving): From Step 4,
$$
13\mid N_t\ \Longleftrightarrow\ 4t+9\equiv 0\pmod{13}\ \Longleftrightarrow\ t\equiv 1\pmod{13}.
$$
Combine this with from Step 3: $t\equiv -38\pmod{121}$.
Since $\gcd(121,13)=1$, the Chinese remainder theorem (theorem Chinese_remainder_theorem) yields a unique class modulo $121\cdot 13=1573$:
$$
t\equiv 1535\pmod{1573}.
$$
Because $n=11^3\cdot 13=1573\cdot 11$, the values $t\in\{0,1,\dots,n-1\}$ with $n\mid N_t$ are exactly
$$
t_j=1535+1573j\quad(j=0,1,\dots,10).
$$
Thus there are $11$ candidates with $n\mid N_t$.

Step 6 (prime_power_lifting_step): Since $11^3\parallel (b-1)$, write $b=1+11^3u$ with $11\nmid u$.
By the first-order binomial expansion theorem first_order_binomial_mod_prime_power,
$$
(1+11^3u)^i\equiv 1+i\cdot 11^3u\pmod{11^4}.
$$
Hence
$$
b^i\equiv 1+i\cdot 11^3u\pmod{11^4}.
$$

Step 7 (prime_power_lifting_step): Substitute from Step 6 into from Step 1:
$$
N_t\equiv \sum_{i=0}^{76}(t+i)+11^3u\sum_{i=0}^{76} i(t+i)\pmod{11^4}.
$$
Now
$$
\sum_{i=0}^{76} i(t+i)=t\sum_{i=0}^{76} i+\sum_{i=0}^{76} i^2.
$$
Compute $\sum i=\frac{76\cdot 77}{2}=2926$ and $\sum i^2=\frac{76\cdot 77\cdot 153}{6}=149226$; both are divisible by $11$.
Therefore $\sum i(t+i)$ is always divisible by $11$, so the second term above is divisible by $11^4$.
Thus, for all $t$,
$$
N_t\equiv \sum_{i=0}^{76}(t+i)=77(t+38)\pmod{11^4}.
In particular, among the candidates $t_j$ from Step 5,
11^4\mid N_{t_j}
\Longleftrightarrow 11^4\mid 77(t_j+38)
\Longleftrightarrow 11^3\mid (t_j+38),
$$
since $v_{11}(77)=1$.
But $t_j+38=1535+38+1573j=1573(j+1)=121\cdot 13\,(j+1)$ has $11^2$ already and gains a third factor of $11$ iff $11\mid (j+1)$, i.e. $j=10$.
So exactly one candidate is $11^4$-bad, namely $t_{10}=17265$.

Step 8 (prime_power_lifting_step): Since $13\parallel(b^2+b+1)$ and $13\nmid(b-1)$ (from Step 4), we have $13\parallel(b^3-1)$.
Reduce $b$ modulo $169$: $b\equiv 21297\equiv 3\pmod{169}$, so $b^3\equiv 27\equiv 1+26\pmod{169}$.
Thus
$$
b^3=1+13w\quad\text{with}\quad 13w\equiv 26\pmod{169},
$$
so $w\equiv 2\pmod{13}$ and in particular $13\nmid w$.
By first_order_binomial_mod_prime_power,
$$
(b^3)^j=(1+13w)^j\equiv 1+13wj\pmod{169}.
$$
Therefore, for $i=3j+r$ with $r\in\{0,1,2\}$,
$$
b^{3j+r}=b^r(b^3)^j\equiv b^r(1+13wj)\pmod{169}.
$$

Step 9 (cycle_decomposition_mod): Using from Step 8 with $13w\equiv 26\pmod{169}$ and $b\equiv 3\pmod{169}$, we get the explicit lifted 3-cycle:
$$
b^{3j}\equiv 1+26j,\quad b^{3j+1}\equiv 3(1+26j),\quad b^{3j+2}\equiv 9(1+26j)\pmod{169}.
$$
Now compute $A$ and $B$ modulo $169$ by a cycle-split.

First, since $L=77=3\cdot 25+2$,
$$
A=\sum_{i=0}^{76} b^i=\sum_{j=0}^{25} b^{3j}+\sum_{j=0}^{25} b^{3j+1}+\sum_{j=0}^{24} b^{3j+2}.
$$
Using the congruences above and $\sum_{j=0}^{25} j=325$, $\sum_{j=0}^{24} j=300$,
$$
\sum_{j=0}^{25} b^{3j}\equiv \sum_{j=0}^{25}(1+26j)=26+26\cdot 325\equiv 26\pmod{169},
$$
$$
\sum_{j=0}^{25} b^{3j+1}\equiv 3\sum_{j=0}^{25}(1+26j)\equiv 3\cdot 26=78\pmod{169},
$$
$$
\sum_{j=0}^{24} b^{3j+2}\equiv 9\sum_{j=0}^{24}(1+26j)=9(25+26\cdot 300)\equiv 9\cdot 51=459\equiv 121\pmod{169}.
$$
Hence $A\equiv 26+78+121=225\equiv 56\pmod{169}$.

Second, compute $B$:
$$
B=\sum_{i=0}^{76} i b^i=\sum_{j=0}^{25} (3j)b^{3j}+\sum_{j=0}^{25}(3j+1)b^{3j+1}+\sum_{j=0}^{24}(3j+2)b^{3j+2}.
$$
Using the lifted 3-cycle just established, note $b^{3j}\equiv 1+26j$, $b^{3j+1}\equiv 3(1+26j)$, and $b^{3j+2}\equiv 9(1+26j)$ modulo $169$.
Write $B\equiv S_0+S_1+S_2\pmod{169}$ where
$$
S_0=\sum_{j=0}^{25} (3j)(1+26j),\quad
S_1=\sum_{j=0}^{25} (3j+1)\cdot 3(1+26j),\quad
S_2=\sum_{j=0}^{24} (3j+2)\cdot 9(1+26j).
$$
Expand each summand to reduce to $\sum j$ and $\sum j^2$:
$$
S_0=\sum_{j=0}^{25}\bigl(3j+78j^2\bigr)=3\sum_{j=0}^{25}j+78\sum_{j=0}^{25}j^2,
$$
$$
S_1=\sum_{j=0}^{25}\bigl(234j^2+87j+3\bigr)=234\sum_{j=0}^{25}j^2+87\sum_{j=0}^{25}j+3\cdot 26,
$$
$$
S_2=\sum_{j=0}^{24}\bigl(702j^2+495j+18\bigr)=702\sum_{j=0}^{24}j^2+495\sum_{j=0}^{24}j+18\cdot 25.
$$
Using $\sum_{j=0}^{25}j=325$, $\sum_{j=0}^{25}j^2=5525$, $\sum_{j=0}^{24}j=300$, $\sum_{j=0}^{24}j^2=4900$, reduce modulo $169$:
$$
S_0=975+78\cdot 5525\equiv 130,\quad
S_1=234\cdot 5525+87\cdot 325+78\equiv 130,\quad
S_2=702\cdot 4900+495\cdot 300+450\equiv 35.
$$
Hence $B\equiv 130+130+35=295\equiv 126\pmod{169}.$
Therefore, from Step 1,
$$
N_t=tA+B\equiv 56t+126\pmod{169}.
Since $\gcd(56,169)=1$, we may invert $56$ modulo $169$, giving the exact criterion
13^2\mid N_t\ \Longleftrightarrow\ 56t+126\equiv 0\pmod{169}\ \Longleftrightarrow\ t\equiv 40\pmod{169}.
$$

Step 10 (linear_congruence_solving): Among the candidates $t_j=1535+1573j$ from Step 5, impose from Step 9:
$$
1535+1573j\equiv 40\pmod{169}.
$$
Reduce modulo $169$: $1535\equiv 14$ and $1573\equiv 52$, so
$$
14+52j\equiv 40\pmod{169}\ \Longleftrightarrow\ 52j\equiv 26\pmod{169}.
Since $\gcd(52,169)=13$, divide by $13$ to get
4j\equiv 2\pmod{13}.
$$
The inverse of $4$ modulo $13$ is $10$, so $j\equiv 20\equiv 7\pmod{13}$, and with $0\le j\le 10$ this forces $j=7$.
Thus exactly one candidate is $13^2$-bad, namely $t_7=12546$, and it is distinct from the $11^4$-bad $t_{10}$ from Step 7.

Step 11 (counting_via_residue_classes): From Step 5 there are $11$ values of $t$ with $n\mid N_t$.
From Step 7 exactly one of these has $11^4\mid N_t$, and from Step 10 exactly one has $13^2\mid N_t$, with no overlap.
Therefore
$$
|S|=11-2=9.
$$

Step 12 (counting_via_residue_classes): By from Step 11, $|S|=9$.
The required normalized output is the unique $N\in\{0,1,\dots,999\}$ with $N\equiv 9\pmod{1000}$, namely $N=009$.

Answer: 009

3. Uniqueness Test
For fixed parameters $(b,p,q,k,L)$, each $t\in\{0,1,\dots,n-1\}$ determines a unique $L$-digit base-$b$ integer $N_t$ by positional expansion, so the set $S$ is well-defined.

From Step 5, the simultaneous conditions $11^3\mid N_t$ and $13\mid N_t$ yield exactly the $11$ candidates $t_j$ in $\{0,\dots,n-1\}$ with $n\mid N_{t_j}$.
From Step 7, $11^4\mid N_{t_j}$ holds for exactly one candidate ($j=10$), and from Step 10, $13^2\mid N_{t_j}$ holds for exactly one candidate ($j=7$), and those indices are different.
Hence exactly $11-2=9$ indices satisfy simultaneously $v_{11}(N_t)=3$ and $v_{13}(N_t)=1$, so the normalized output is uniquely forced to be $009$.

9
